mnemonics.title: LPS Meaning
mnemonics.content: LPS = Longest Proper Prefix which is also Suffix - Remember: 'Proper' means NOT the whole string, 'Prefix' from start, 'Suffix' from end - they must match!

mnemonics.title: KMP Working
mnemonics.content: When mismatch occurs: Don't go back in text, use LPS to jump in pattern - Think: 'Slide smart, don't restart!'

mnemonics.title: LPS Computation
mnemonics.content: If match: increment; if mismatch and len>0: fallback to LPS[len-1]; if mismatch and len=0: set 0 and move on - Think: 'Match grow, mismatch fallback, zero stay zero!'

acronyms.usage: The famous string matching algorithm discovered in 1977

acronyms.expansion: Longest Proper Prefix which is also Suffix
acronyms.usage: Array storing the longest prefix that is also a suffix for each position in pattern

acronyms.expansion: Length of text (string to be searched)
acronyms.usage: In KMP time complexity O(n + m)

acronyms.expansion: Length of pattern (string to search for)
acronyms.usage: In KMP time complexity O(n + m)

visualTips.visualization: Think of LPS array as a 'cheat sheet' that tells you: 'If mismatch happens here, jump back to this position in the pattern' - like a GPS recalculating route
Visualize the pattern as a 'window' sliding over the text - KMP tells you exactly how far to slide without missing any potential matches
Draw the pattern and highlight overlapping parts: if prefix ABC matches suffix ABC at the end, then ABC is a prefix-suffix - LPS stores its length

analogies.analogy: Naive is like re-reading the entire book every time you miss a word; KMP is like using bookmarks and knowing where to look next without starting over.
LPS is like a 'reference guide' for the pattern - it tells you 'if you fail here, don't start from scratch, start from where this prefix ends'.
Preprocessing in KMP is like studying the pattern before searching - like memorizing key words in a document before looking for them.
